tipping_points.port_congestion_note: Rotterdam and Hamburg facing 15–25% longer dwell times due to rerouted volumes

foods.name: Shrimp

drivers.description: Farmed shrimp from SE Asia typically routes via Suez — countries that import it are most affected; local producers face lower exposure
Red Sea transit insurance up 15× since Nov 2023
Extra 10–14 days via Cape of Good Hope adds refrigeration costs

foods.explanation: Farmed shrimp from Vietnam and Thailand is one of the most Suez-dependent proteins for importing nations. Countries that produce their own shrimp (India, Thailand domestically) face much lower exposure than those relying on long-haul imports.

foods.name: Canned Tuna

drivers.description: Thai and Philippine canneries export exclusively in 20/40ft containers
Vessel insurance for Red Sea routing has surged 15× since late 2023
Cape route adds 14 days and roughly 40% extra bunker fuel per voyage

foods.explanation: Thailand processes over a third of the world's canned tuna. Every can in European stores was packed into a container that once sailed past Bab-el-Mandeb — now rerouted around Africa.

foods.name: Salmon

drivers.description: Norwegian salmon to Asia routes disrupted; some supply reallocation hits Europe
Perishable cargo commands higher war-risk premiums

foods.explanation: European farmed salmon mostly travels short intra-European routes unaffected by Suez, but global supply reallocation and higher packaging import costs add modest pressure.

foods.name: Canned Sardines

drivers.description: Moroccan and Portuguese sardines exported in steel cans, some via Suez lanes
Mediterranean-origin vessels still pay elevated war-risk premiums
Suez congestion affects Mediterranean export timing

foods.explanation: Moroccan sardines dominate European canned seafood imports. The Atlantic-to-Mediterranean leg is shorter, but elevated insurance and port congestion still filter through.

foods.name: Cod

drivers.description: Frozen cod from Iceland and Norway shipped in reefer containers
Reefer container premiums have risen with Red Sea risk

foods.explanation: North Atlantic cod rarely transits Suez, but reefer container spot rates have risen globally as capacity is absorbed by rerouting around Africa.

foods.name: Chicken

drivers.description: Polystyrene trays and plastic wrap sourced from SE Asia
Haulage and port handling costs rise with global freight inflation

foods.explanation: Chicken is mostly produced locally in Europe with domestic feed, insulating it from container shipping costs. Indirect exposure comes from imported packaging and processing equipment.

foods.name: Beef

drivers.description: Some seasoning and packaging sourced via long-haul container routes
Global container market tightness raises even non-Suez transport costs

foods.explanation: European beef production is largely self-contained. The Red Sea crisis has minimal direct impact; modest cost pass-through reflects imported packaging and equipment parts.

foods.name: Pork

drivers.description: Packaging and some feed additives travel in containers
Wider freight market inflation touches pork supply chain margins

foods.explanation: Pork production in Europe relies on domestic feed crops and local abattoirs. Container-route disruption has minimal direct effect.

foods.name: Lamb

drivers.description: New Zealand lamb to Europe was partly routed via Suez
NZ lamb ships pay elevated insurance for Red Sea passage

foods.explanation: New Zealand lamb exports to Europe historically routed via Suez for speed; Cape rerouting adds two weeks and ~$400/container in fuel costs, partially passed on at retail.

foods.name: Turkey

drivers.description: Plastic and film packaging sourced from Asian suppliers
Elevated global container rates add indirect cost pressure

foods.explanation: Turkey is produced domestically across Europe. Container-route disruption affects imported packaging and specialty seasoning more than the protein itself.

foods.name: Sausages

drivers.description: Spices, casings and specialty ingredients from Asia and East Africa
Imported ingredient containers face higher insurance

foods.explanation: Sausages contain a diverse supply chain: casings from China, spices from East Africa and Asia, all moving through or near the Bab-el-Mandeb strait.

foods.name: Milk

drivers.description: Tetra Pak materials and plastics partially sourced from Asia
Wider container market inflation touches packaging supply chains

foods.explanation: Fresh milk is nearly entirely local. The Red Sea crisis reaches it only through imported Tetra Pak materials and other packaging components.

foods.name: Cheese

drivers.description: Some specialty cheeses packaged with imported materials
Global container inflation adds marginal cost

foods.explanation: European cheese production is predominantly local with domestic milk. Minimal exposure to Red Sea disruption beyond packaging costs.

foods.name: Butter

drivers.description: New Zealand butter exports routed Cape due to Red Sea
NZ vessels pay elevated premiums for former Suez routing

foods.explanation: New Zealand is Europe's largest butter import source. Cape rerouting has added weeks to delivery times and elevated spot prices modestly.

foods.name: Yoghurt

drivers.description: Polypropylene cups and lids sourced from Asian manufacturers
Container market tightness adds indirect cost

foods.explanation: Yoghurt is produced locally from domestic milk. Container disruption touches it mainly through imported pot and lid manufacturing.

foods.name: Eggs

drivers.description: Cardboard trays use pulp partly sourced from SE Asian mills
Marginal cost increase from global container market

foods.explanation: Eggs are one of the most locally-produced foods in Europe. Red Sea disruption has negligible direct impact.

foods.name: Cream

drivers.description: UHT cream packaging materials from Asian suppliers
Global container rates add marginal cost

foods.explanation: Cream is produced from domestic milk. Container shipping costs affect mainly packaging inputs.

foods.name: Bread

drivers.description: Polyethylene bags and wrapping sourced from Asian factories
Global container market tightness adds indirect cost

foods.explanation: Bread is baked locally using European wheat, largely insulating it from Suez shipping disruption. Imported packaging is the main exposure point.

foods.name: Rice

drivers.description: Asian rice — Thai jasmine, Indian basmati — ships entirely in containers via Suez
Rice vessels from India, Pakistan and Thailand face elevated Red Sea insurance
Cape rerouting adds 10–14 days to rice voyages from South Asia

foods.explanation: European rice imports are dominated by Asian varieties (basmati, jasmine) that transit the Bab-el-Mandeb. Every bag of imported rice in a supermarket crossed the Red Sea shipping corridor.

foods.name: Pasta

drivers.description: Semolina from North Africa and packaging from Asia travel partially via Suez
Mediterranean supply chains face elevated insurance costs

foods.explanation: Italian and European pasta is made from locally grown durum wheat, but some North African semolina and Asian packaging components add modest Suez exposure.

foods.name: Flour

drivers.description: Multi-wall paper bags partially sourced from overseas mills
Global freight inflation adds marginal cost

foods.explanation: European flour is milled from locally grown wheat. Red Sea disruption affects it mainly through imported packaging materials.

foods.name: Oats

drivers.description: Cardboard oat packaging partially sourced from Asian mills
Container market tightness adds indirect cost

foods.explanation: Oats are grown and processed in Europe. Modest exposure comes through imported packaging and specialty oat varieties from North America.

foods.name: Cornflakes

drivers.description: Corn syrup, vitamins and specialty coatings often sourced from Asia
Food additive imports face elevated container insurance

foods.explanation: Cornflakes are packaged locally but rely on imported corn syrup concentrates, vitamins and food colorings that route via container through Suez.

foods.name: Couscous

drivers.description: North African couscous exports route partly via Mediterranean/Suez lanes
Mediterranean exporters pay elevated insurance premiums
Suez congestion affects North Africa–Europe trade timing

foods.explanation: Couscous is imported from North Africa (Morocco, Algeria) and the Middle East. The short Suez-adjacent route means elevated insurance is the primary transmission mechanism.

foods.name: Instant Noodles

drivers.description: Instant noodles are manufactured in SE Asia and exported in high volumes via Suez
Asian food exporters face steeply higher Red Sea premiums
Cape rerouting adds 2+ weeks to Asian noodle deliveries

foods.explanation: Instant noodles are an extreme case: manufactured in bulk in Thailand, Indonesia and China, packed in containers, and shipped directly through the Bab-el-Mandeb corridor to Europe.

foods.name: Crackers

drivers.description: Palm oil, sesame and specialty flavourings from SE Asia
SE Asian ingredient containers face higher insurance

foods.explanation: Crackers manufactured in Europe still import palm oil, sesame seeds and some coatings from SE Asian suppliers, all routed via the Suez corridor.

foods.name: Tomatoes

drivers.description: Plastic packaging and transport trays from Asian manufacturers
Global container rates affect packaging cost

foods.explanation: European tomatoes are grown domestically or in North Africa. The Red Sea crisis affects mainly packaging components sourced from Asia.

foods.name: Potatoes

drivers.description: Polypropylene bags and plastic film from Asian producers
Marginal container cost increase

foods.explanation: Potatoes are one of Europe's most locally-produced foods. Essentially no direct Red Sea exposure.

foods.name: Onions

drivers.description: Net bags and packaging materials from Asian suppliers
Marginal impact from container market

foods.explanation: Onions are produced domestically across Europe and North Africa. Red Sea disruption has negligible impact.

foods.name: Bananas

drivers.description: Some banana shipments from East Africa routed via Red Sea
Reefer vessels face elevated insurance for Red Sea-adjacent routing

foods.explanation: European bananas come mainly from Latin America (unaffected), but East African banana imports (Kenya, Tanzania) transit the Bab-el-Mandeb, adding modest upward pressure on blended prices.

foods.name: Apples

drivers.description: Protective foam netting and packaging from Asian manufacturers
Marginal impact from container market

foods.explanation: European apples are grown in Italy, France, Poland and Spain. Essentially no Suez dependency.

foods.name: Oranges

drivers.description: South African and Egyptian oranges routed near Bab-el-Mandeb
Southern African reefer ships face elevated insurance

foods.explanation: South African citrus exports to Europe previously used the Suez Canal; many vessels now reroute via Cape Town, adding costs absorbed in part by European supermarkets.

foods.name: Lettuce

drivers.description: Clamshell packaging partially from Asian producers
Minimal indirect container cost

foods.explanation: Lettuce is perishable and almost entirely local. The Red Sea crisis has essentially no impact.

foods.name: Bell Peppers

drivers.description: Spanish and Dutch greenhouse peppers use Asian-sourced packaging
Marginal container cost increase

foods.explanation: Bell peppers are grown in Spain and the Netherlands. Red Sea disruption affects mainly imported packaging.

foods.name: Carrots

drivers.description: Polypropylene bags from Asian manufacturers
Negligible container market impact

foods.explanation: Carrots are produced locally across Europe. Minimal Red Sea exposure.

foods.name: Avocado

drivers.description: East African avocados (Kenya, Tanzania) route via Bab-el-Mandeb
Reefer vessels from East Africa face elevated insurance
Some Kenyan avocados rerouted around Cape, adding 10 days

foods.explanation: Europe imports avocados from both Americas (low Suez exposure) and East Africa (high Suez exposure). The blended effect is moderate — Kenyan exports transit directly past the Houthi attack zone.

foods.name: Garlic

drivers.description: China accounts for ~75% of global garlic exports, shipping via Suez to Europe
Chinese food exports face elevated Red Sea insurance
Cape rerouting from China to Europe adds 12–14 days

foods.explanation: China dominates global garlic production and exports directly to European supermarkets via container ship through the Red Sea. Higher than most produce.

foods.name: Cucumber

drivers.description: Film packaging and trays from Asian suppliers
Marginal container cost increase

foods.explanation: Cucumbers are greenhouse-grown in the Netherlands and Spain. Essentially no Suez route dependency.

foods.name: Frozen Peas

drivers.description: Frozen vegetables shipped in refrigerated containers partly via Suez
Reefer containers face elevated insurance
Cape rerouting adds costs for Asian and Indian frozen pea exporters

foods.explanation: India is a major frozen pea exporter to Europe. Indian frozen vegetable containers transit the Bab-el-Mandeb — now rerouted around Africa at extra cost.

foods.name: Cooking Oil

drivers.description: Palm oil from Malaysia/Indonesia bulk ships and containers route via Suez
Palm oil carriers face elevated Red Sea premiums
Cape rerouting adds 14+ days from SE Asian palm oil ports

foods.explanation: Palm oil — used in virtually all cooking oils — is produced almost exclusively in Malaysia and Indonesia. Every tanker bound for European refineries must navigate near the Red Sea conflict zone.

foods.name: Frozen Pizza

drivers.description: Frozen pizza imports and ingredient components in reefer containers
Refrigerated containers attract elevated premiums
Extra transit time increases refrigeration costs

foods.explanation: Frozen pizza combines multiple imported ingredients — palm oil, mozzarella ingredients, tomato paste concentrate — many of which move in containers via the Suez corridor.

foods.name: Chocolate

drivers.description: Cocoa from Ivory Coast (Atlantic route) and Asian chocolate imports
Specialty chocolate from SE Asia and Middle East faces higher insurance
Some shipping lane diversions affect ingredient timing

foods.explanation: West African cocoa (Ivory Coast, Ghana) travels the Atlantic — mostly unaffected. But Asian confectionery imports, cocoa butter from Indonesia, and specialty chocolate all route via Suez, contributing to a moderate blended exposure.

foods.name: Crisps / Chips

drivers.description: SE Asian palm oil for frying and imported Asian crisps ship via Suez
Bulk vegetable oil carriers face elevated Red Sea premiums
Palm oil from Indonesia/Malaysia adds 2 weeks via Cape

foods.explanation: Crisps depend on palm oil for frying — sourced almost entirely from Malaysia and Indonesia. This single ingredient makes crisps highly exposed to Red Sea disruption.

foods.name: Biscuits / Cookies

drivers.description: Palm oil, cocoa and Asian-packaged biscuit imports via Suez
Food commodity vessels face elevated insurance
SE Asian ingredient suppliers rerouted around Cape

foods.explanation: Biscuits are heavy consumers of palm oil and often include imported ingredients (cocoa powder from SE Asia, specialty flavourings) that transit the Bab-el-Mandeb.

foods.name: Soft Drinks

drivers.description: High-fructose corn syrup, citric acid, and colouring from Asia/Americas via Suez
Container-shipped syrups and concentrates face elevated insurance

foods.explanation: Soft drink concentrates and ingredient syrups are often produced in the US, Asia and Middle East and shipped to European bottling plants in containers via the Suez route.

foods.name: Beer

drivers.description: Specialty hops from New Zealand, malt from Australia ship via long routes
Global container market tightness adds indirect cost

foods.explanation: Most European beer uses local barley and hops. Craft breweries importing specialty hops from New Zealand or Australia, and imported canned beers from Asia, carry higher exposure.

foods.name: Coffee

drivers.description: Ethiopian, Yemeni and Vietnamese coffee beans ship directly through Bab-el-Mandeb
East African coffee exporters face highest Red Sea insurance premiums
Ethiopia and Yemen exports previously used short Red Sea route

foods.explanation: Coffee is one of the most Red Sea-exposed foods. Ethiopian and Yemeni coffee — considered premium — ships directly from ports at the Red Sea entrance. Vietnamese robusta (the world's #2 bean) also passes through Bab-el-Mandeb.

foods.name: Sugar

drivers.description: Cane sugar from India, Pakistan and East Africa routes via Suez
Sugar carriers from South Asia face elevated Red Sea premiums
Indian and Pakistani sugar exports rerouted around Africa

foods.explanation: India and Pakistan are major sugar exporters to Europe. Their shipping lanes pass directly through the Bab-el-Mandeb, making sugar moderately exposed to Red Sea freight disruption.

foods.name: Peanut Butter

drivers.description: Chinese and SE Asian peanuts and peanut butter ship via Suez in bulk
Commodity food vessels from Asia face elevated insurance
Chinese peanut exports via Cape add 12–14 days

foods.explanation: Peanut butter production relies on peanuts from China (the world's largest producer) and Argentina (Atlantic route). The Chinese supply chain is heavily Suez-dependent.

foods.name: Orange Juice

drivers.description: South African and Indian OJ concentrate ships via Bab-el-Mandeb
OJ tankers from South Africa and India pay elevated premiums

foods.explanation: Brazilian OJ concentrate takes the Atlantic route — unaffected. But South African and Indian OJ, plus some concentrate from Egypt, travels via the Suez corridor, adding modest blended exposure.

foods.name: Mayonnaise

drivers.description: Soybean oil from South America and egg powder from SE Asia via various routes
Some ingredient containers route via Suez

foods.explanation: Mayonnaise relies on vegetable oil (partly imported palm or soy from SE Asia) and egg powder, with moderate Suez route dependency.

foods.name: Tomato Sauce

drivers.description: Tomato concentrate and packaging from SE Asia and North Africa
Some ingredient containers route via Mediterranean/Suez lanes

foods.explanation: Tomato sauce ingredients (concentrate, spices, packaging) partly imported from North Africa and Asia via Suez-adjacent routes.

foods.name: Baby Formula

drivers.description: Australian and NZ whey, and Asian formula (Danone/Nestlé Asia) all route via Suez
High-value food cargo commands elevated war-risk insurance
NZ and Australian dairy container ships now reroute via Cape

foods.explanation: Baby formula is heavily imported from Australia, New Zealand and Asian manufacturing hubs. NZ dairy containers previously transited Suez for speed; Cape rerouting adds 12 days and significant cost.

foods.name: Lentils

drivers.description: Canadian lentils (world #1 exporter) ship via Atlantic to Europe; Indian lentils via Suez
Indian and Turkish lentil exports face elevated Red Sea insurance

foods.explanation: Canada and Australia supply most of Europe's lentils via Atlantic and Pacific routes (low Suez exposure). Indian lentil imports carry moderate exposure, giving a blended low result.

foods.name: Chickpeas

drivers.description: Australian chickpeas via Pacific; Indian and Turkish via Suez
Indian chickpea exports face elevated Bab-el-Mandeb premiums

foods.explanation: Chickpeas come from both Australia (Cape or Pacific route) and India/Turkey (Suez route). Blended exposure is low.

foods.name: Tofu

drivers.description: Soy protein and pre-made tofu from China and SE Asia via Suez
Chinese and SE Asian food exports face elevated insurance
Chinese tofu and soy ingredients rerouted via Cape

foods.explanation: Specialty tofu and soy protein concentrate imported from China and Japan must navigate the Red Sea container corridor, giving tofu moderate Suez exposure.

foods.name: Dried Beans

drivers.description: Some bean varieties (black beans, kidney beans) imported from SE Asia and East Africa
Container market tightness adds indirect cost

foods.explanation: Most European beans (haricot, flageolet) are grown domestically or in the Americas. Specialty imported varieties carry some Suez exposure.

foods.name: Quinoa

drivers.description: Peruvian and Bolivian quinoa ships via Atlantic to Europe
Global container market tightness affects even non-Suez routes

foods.explanation: Quinoa is produced in Peru and Bolivia and ships via the Atlantic — largely unaffected by the Red Sea crisis. Modest exposure reflects global container market tightness.

foods.name: Olive Oil

drivers.description: Mediterranean-origin olive oil uses very short shipping routes
Marginal global container inflation impact

foods.explanation: Olive oil is produced in Spain, Italy, Greece and Tunisia and shipped via short Mediterranean routes that largely bypass the Bab-el-Mandeb.
